description: Ordering food, asking about the menu, and dining out

items.notes: Standard polite request when entering a restaurant. 'Kahdelle' is the allative form of 'kaksi'.
Polite request. 'Ruokalista' is a compound of 'ruoka' (food) + 'lista' (list). 'Saanko' is a polite way to ask permission.
Formal/plural 'te' form, appropriate for addressing restaurant staff. 'Suosittelette' is the te-form of 'suositella'.
Conditional form 'haluaisin' is more polite than 'haluan' (I want). Standard way to signal readiness to order.
Simple and direct way to order while pointing at a menu item. Very common in casual dining.
Straightforward order. Finns are among the world's biggest coffee drinkers — ordering coffee is extremely common.
Very polite conditional form. 'Voisinko' (could I) is the conditional of 'voida' + question suffix '-ko'. 'Vettä' is the partitive of 'vesi'. In Finland, tap water is free and excellent quality.
Formal 'te' form ('teillä' = at you, formal/plural). 'Kasvisruoka' is a compound: 'kasvis' (vegetable) + 'ruoka' (food). Partitive 'kasvisruokaa' used in questions about existence.
Important safety phrase. 'Pähkinöille' is the allative plural of 'pähkinä'. Always inform staff about allergies in Finland — they take this seriously.
'Ilman' (without) takes the partitive case: 'maitoa'. Useful when ordering coffee or tea.
Quick way to duplicate a companion's order. 'Minulle' is the allative of 'minä'.
Polite conditional form of 'saada'. In Finland, the bill is usually not brought until requested. Tipping is not expected but rounding up is appreciated.
Finland is very card-friendly — almost everywhere accepts card payments, even small amounts.
'Hyvää' is partitive of 'hyvä', used because 'ruoka' is an uncountable/mass concept here. A nice compliment to give.
'Herkullista' is the partitive of 'herkullinen'. A stronger compliment than 'hyvää'. Finns appreciate sincere but brief compliments about food.
Important dietary question. 'Gluteeniton' follows the Finnish '-ton/-tön' suffix pattern meaning 'without'. Finland has good awareness of celiac disease and gluten-free options are widely available.
Polite conditional 'haluaisin'. 'Leipää' is partitive of 'leipä'. Bread is a staple at Finnish meals — rye bread (ruisleipä) is the most traditional.
Polite way to flag a mistake. 'Tilaukseni' = 'tilaus' (order) + possessive suffix '-ni' (my). Finnish negation uses 'ei' + verb stem.
Useful when someone else's dish looks appealing or when offered a sample. 'Maistaa' means to taste or try food.
'Olutta' is the partitive singular of 'olut', used after numbers. Finland has a thriving craft beer scene alongside traditional lagers.

items.english: Do you have a lunch menu?
items.notes: Formal 'te' form. 'Lounaslista' is a compound: 'lounas' (lunch) + 'lista' (list). Many Finnish restaurants offer affordable set lunch menus ('lounas') on weekdays, typically 10–15 euros including salad, bread, drink, and coffee.

items.english: What is the soup of the day?
items.notes: 'Päivän' is the genitive of 'päivä' (day). Soup of the day is a very common lunch option in Finnish restaurants and workplace cafeterias.
'Jälkiruoka' is a compound: 'jälki' (after) + 'ruoka' (food). The accusative form 'jälkiruoan' indicates a specific dessert.
Elative case used after 'kiitos' to express what you are thankful for: 'hyvästä' (from 'hyvä') and 'ruoasta' (from 'ruoka'). A warm way to thank the host or staff.

items.english: Could we sit on the terrace?
items.notes: 'Voisimmeko' is the conditional 'we' form of 'voida' + question suffix. 'Terassille' is the allative of 'terassi'. Terrace dining is cherished during Finnish summers when daylight lasts very long.

'Palveluraha' is a compound: 'palvelu' (service) + 'raha' (money). In Finland, service charge is typically included in the price and tipping is not customary, though it is appreciated for exceptional service.

items.english: Would you like something to drink?
items.notes: Formal 'te' form — a question the waiter might ask you. 'Juotavaa' is the partitive of the verbal noun 'juotava' (something drinkable), derived from 'juoda' (to drink).

items.english: One glass of red wine, please.
items.notes: 'Punaviini' is a compound: 'puna' (red) + 'viini' (wine). 'Punaviiniä' is the partitive, used when ordering a portion of something.
